name: Mother Shipton Prophecies
description: The Mother Shipton Prophecies are a collection of purported predictions attributed to Ursula Southeil (c. 1488-1561), better known as Mother Shipton, a supposed prophetess from Yorkshire, England. Proponents claim she accurately foresaw major historical events, including the Great Fire of London, the invention of iron ships, the defeat of the Spanish Armada, and even the end of the world. These prophecies are primarily circulated in rhyming verse, often presented as direct quotes from Mother Shipton. The most famous versions originated in a pamphlet published in 1862 by Charles Hindley, who later admitted to fabricating many of the prophecies. Mainstream historians and folklorists generally view the prophecies as largely apocryphal, citing the lack of contemporary evidence for Mother Shipton's prophetic abilities and the strong evidence of later embellishments and outright fabrications. The prophecies' enduring popularity reflects a fascination with divination and the anxieties of different historical periods, with new verses often appearing to address contemporary concerns. The historical Ursula Southeil likely existed, but her transformation into a legendary prophetess is a product of later folklore and literary invention. Scholarly analysis focuses on tracing the evolution of the prophecies and their role in shaping popular perceptions of the future.
subtitle: Legendary prophecies of Ursula Southeil.

claims.claim: Mother Shipton predicted the Great Fire of London.
claims.evidence: Verses within the 1862 Hindley pamphlet describe a great fire consuming London.
claims.criticism: The 1862 pamphlet is not a reliable source, as Hindley admitted to fabricating much of its content. No contemporary accounts mention this prophecy.

claims.claim: She foresaw the invention of iron ships and the use of fossil fuels.
claims.evidence: The prophecies mention 'carriages without horses shall go' and 'iron shall float in the water'.
claims.criticism: These verses are vague and open to interpretation. Similar predictions can be found in other prophetic traditions, and their accuracy is often attributed to coincidence or hindsight bias. The 1862 Hindley version is the primary source.

claims.claim: Mother Shipton predicted the end of the world in 1881.
claims.evidence: Some versions of the prophecies, particularly those circulating in the late 19th century, included a specific end-date.
claims.criticism: The 1881 prediction proved false, discrediting the prophecies in the eyes of many. This specific prophecy is not found in earlier versions and is likely a later addition to capitalize on millennial anxieties.

keyFigures.name: Charles Hindley
keyFigures.role: Publisher of the 1862 pamphlet, fabricator of prophecies.

keyFigures.name: Richard Head
keyFigures.role: Author of the earliest known biography of Mother Shipton (1667)

timeline.event: Earliest known biography of Mother Shipton published by Richard Head.
Charles Hindley publishes his version of the prophecies, containing many fabrications.
The predicted end of the world fails to occur.
The Mother Shipton Prophecies continue to be circulated and reinterpreted.

sources.author: Hindley, Charles
sources.work: The Life and Prophecies of Ursula Sontheil, better known as Mother Shipton
sources.title: Hindley, Charles

sources.author: Head, Richard
